prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden inability to close the right eye and a drooping mouth suggests a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which commonly follows a viral illness and affects the whole side of the face including the forehead. This is less likely to be a stroke because strokes usually spare the forehead and cause additional signs like arm/leg weakness, trouble speaking or severe headache, which you don’t have. You should protect the eye (use lubricating drops by day, ointment and patch at night) and see a clinician promptly because starting oral steroids within about 72 hours improves the chance of full recovery. Go to the ER only if you develop limb weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, confusion, worsening vision, or rapidly progressing symptoms.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided facial weakness with inability to close the right eye and mouth droop most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), especially with a recent viral illness and no arm/leg weakness or speech problems. Peripheral palsy affects the whole side of the face including eye closure; a stroke more often spares the forehead and usually has other neurological signs. You still need a medical exam to confirm the cause, start treatment if indicated (steroids within 72 hours can help), and get instructions to protect the eye from drying. Go to the ER right away if you develop limb weakness, facial numbness spread, trouble speaking/understanding, severe headache, vision loss, or worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-side face weakness that includes inability to close the eye and drooping of the mouth most likely represents a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). The recent mild cold supports a viral-related nerve inflammation. Bell’s palsy causes weakness of the whole half of the face (including the forehead), whereas a stroke often spares forehead movement and usually has other signs like arm/leg weakness, numbness, or speech problems. You still need a medical exam to confirm the cause, start treatment (steroids work best if begun within ~72 hours), and get instructions to protect the eye (lubrication/patch) to avoid corneal injury.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have a sudden peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). It often follows a viral illness and causes weakness of the whole one side of the face so you can’t fully close the eye and the mouth droops. A stroke is less likely because you have no arm/leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding, and no severe headache. Bell’s palsy is usually treated promptly with oral steroids and with measures to protect the eye (drops/patch) to prevent damage. See a doctor soon to confirm the diagnosis and start treatment.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Sudden one-sided facial weakness that includes inability to close the eye and mouth droop is most often a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy), especially after a recent viral cold. Bell’s palsy usually affects the whole half of the face, while strokes often spare the forehead and cause arm/leg weakness or speech problems — which you do not have. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within ~72 hours) can improve recovery, and the eye needs protection (lubricating drops, patch) if it can’t fully close. Because some strokes can look similar, you still need a prompt medical exam to confirm the diagnosis and start treatment if appropriate.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You likely have Bell’s palsy — a sudden weakness of the facial nerve that often follows viral colds and affects the whole side of the face, including inability to close the eye and drooping of the mouth. A stroke more commonly spares the forehead and usually causes other problems like arm/leg weakness or trouble speaking, which you do not have. Bell’s palsy should be evaluated promptly because steroid treatment works best if started within about 72 hours, and the eye needs protection (lubricating drops, patching) to prevent corneal injury. Your doctor can exam you, consider starting steroids, advise eye care, and order imaging only if symptoms are atypical or worsen.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have Bell’s palsy, which is inflammation of the facial nerve that often follows a viral cold and causes sudden one-sided facial weakness (drooping mouth, trouble closing the eye). The absence of arm/leg weakness, speech or comprehension problems makes a stroke less likely, though not impossible. Early treatment—usually oral steroids—works best if started quickly, and eye care is important to prevent drying/injury. Get evaluated soon so a clinician can confirm the cause, start treatment if needed, and show how to protect the eye. If you develop limb weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, severe headache, or rapidly worsening symptoms, seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided face weakness that includes inability to fully close the eye and drooping of the mouth most commonly means a peripheral facial nerve problem (Bell’s palsy), often linked to a recent viral illness. That fits because Bell’s palsy affects the whole side of the face, including the forehead. A stroke more often leaves the forehead working and usually causes arm/leg weakness, numbness, trouble speaking or vision changes — which you do not have. Even with likely Bell’s palsy, early treatment (usually steroids) and eye protection to prevent corneal damage are important.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy) — inflammation of the facial nerve that can follow a viral cold and causes weakness of the whole one side of the face (can’t close the eye, mouth droop). The lack of arm/leg weakness, speech trouble, or severe headache makes a stroke less likely, but stroke can sometimes cause isolated facial weakness. You should be seen promptly so a clinician can confirm the cause, consider starting steroids (most effective if begun within ~72 hours), and advise eye protection (artificial tears during the day, ointment/taping at night) to prevent corneal damage. Go to the ER right away if you develop weakness elsewhere, trouble speaking/seeing, numbness, or severe headache.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): The sudden weakness on one side of your face that prevents closing the eye and causes mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble, most commonly means a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy). That often follows a recent viral illness and affects the whole side of the face, including the forehead (you can’t close the eye). It is less likely to be a stroke because strokes often spare the forehead and usually cause limb or speech problems. Treatment (steroids) works best if started early, and eye care is important because you can’t fully close the eye.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 75%
